what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard block item that records a medium-severity development risk: Obsidian-specific plugins and syntax (Bases, Dataview, callouts, comments, block references, embeds) may leak into the canonical markdown bodies of items, turning portable markdown into an Obsidian dialect and making the system dependent on Obsidian for correct rendering.
observations: YAML frontmatter declares id item-a385f1ca, address board://item-a385f1ca, type block, source claude_code, state current, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-48cd6801.
Title labels the item 'P11 · 3.1 Plugin lock-in & Obsidian-specific syntax leaking into canonical dat' with an apparent truncation at 'dat'.
Body is a risk note under heading '### 3.1 Plugin lock-in & Obsidian-specific syntax leaking into canonical data (MEDIUM)'.
Lists concrete Obsidian-only constructs: .base files, dataview code blocks, callouts, %%comments%%, ^block-id anchors, and ![[...]] embeds.
Argues that if these constructs enter item bodies, the canonical data becomes Obsidian-dialect markdown that other surfaces must parse or strip.
Frames the risk as a slow inversion where the 'second lens' becomes load-bearing rather than Obsidian being declared master.
History shows a single 'filed' transition by author_session ch-3mpkjg3r at 2026-06-24T05:12:11.146595+00:00.
standouts: The core warning: convenience features can silently accrete Obsidian-only constructs into canonical data.
The 'second lens becomes load-bearing' metaphor for gradual tool lock-in.
A specific syntax inventory that defines the leakage surface.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item schema with YAML metadata, id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses a markdown heading with a numbered section and severity tag.
Uses inline code/backticks to quote Obsidian syntax examples, keeping them as references rather than active syntax.
Links to a parent item via the part_of relationship.
concerns: Title appears truncated ('canonical dat') due to YAML line wrapping.
Severity is asserted in the title but not backed by evidence inside this file.
No mitigation or action items are recorded here.
The term 'phone surface' is used without definition.
notes: A risk-register entry warning that Obsidian-only syntax can quietly couple canonical item bodies to Obsidian if authors use its convenience features.
questions: What is the 'phone surface' and what rendering constraints does it impose?
What is the parent item-48cd6801 and how does this risk fit into it?
Where is the boundary between acceptable Obsidian features and leakage?
How is 'clean portable markdown' formally defined and validated?
Are there existing linting or sanitization rules to prevent these constructs from entering item bodies?
purpose_vs_actual: The title and body both present a risk item, and the file does exactly that; purpose matches content.
apparent_themes: Separation of storage format from presentation tool
intention_signals: It is trying to raise awareness that adopting Obsidian's mature features for editing convenience can inadvertently make Obsidian's dialect part of the canonical data model, so the team can guard the canonical format before the dependency becomes structural.
novelty: It characterizes lock-in as a slow data-accretion process rather than an explicit architectural decision, and it inventories specific Obsidian-only syntax constructs that constitute the leakage surface.
contribution: A concrete, named inventory of Obsidian-specific constructs and a clear narrative of how convenience-driven editing can silently couple canonical data to a tool, which a fused system would keep as a guardrail for format neutrality.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard risk item documenting medium-severity concern that Obsidian-specific plugins and syntax may leak into canonical markdown item bodies, gradually making the data dependent on Obsidian rendering.
intention_for_embedding: To flag and document the mechanism of slow plugin lock-in so the team can preserve portable canonical markdown.
